name: Mysore Pak
description: Mysore Pak is a legendary South Indian melt-in-the-mouth sweet made with just three main ingredients — gram flour (besan), sugar and ghee. Originating from the royal kitchens of Mysore, this fudgy, soft barfi has a rich golden colour and an irresistible aroma of roasted besan and pure ghee. It is a must-make during Diwali and special occasions, and though it looks simple, getting the perfect soft texture requires attention to sugar syrup consistency and timing.

ingredients.name: Oil
ingredients.notes: optional, mixed with ghee for softer texture

steps.instruction: Grease a square or rectangular plate generously with ghee and set aside.
Heat 1 tsp ghee in a non-stick pan. Add besan flour and roast on low-medium flame for 3 to 4 minutes until a nice aroma comes. Do not brown.
Sieve the roasted besan once to remove lumps and ensure fine texture.
In a bowl, mix ¾ cup ghee with the roasted besan to form a smooth, slightly pouring consistency mixture. Set aside for 15 minutes.
In a non-stick pan, add 1 cup sugar and ¼ cup water. Heat on medium flame, stirring until sugar dissolves.
Bring to a boil and cook until one-string consistency is reached — when you take the syrup between thumb and index finger, a single string should form.
steps.tip: The two critical steps are: checking one-string consistency of sugar syrup and the final Mysore Pak consistency.

steps.instruction: Add the besan-ghee mixture to the sugar syrup. Mix well until combined.
Add the remaining ¼ cup ghee in intervals, stirring continuously. Keep the flame on low.
steps.tip: Add ghee in portions, not all at once — the mixture absorbs ghee quickly.

steps.instruction: The mixture will first stick to the pan while scraping with the ladle. Keep stirring and adding ghee little by little.
steps.tip: Keep the flame on low after adding besan to prevent burning.

steps.instruction: At one stage, the mixture will come together as a ball without sticking at all. It will not be sticky anymore. This is the perfect stage — switch off immediately.
steps.tip: Switch off at the exact moment the mixture leaves the pan and forms a non-sticky ball — even a minute late will make it hard.

steps.instruction: Pour the mixture into the greased plate. Do not flatten — it will spread beautifully by itself.
While still hot, cut into squares or diamonds using a greased knife.
Let it cool completely. The ghee that oozes out will be fully absorbed as it cools.
steps.tip: Ghee may ooze out slightly at the final stage — this is normal and will be absorbed on cooling.

steps.instruction: Remove pieces gently and store in an airtight container.
steps.tip: Mysore Pak stays well at room temperature for 5 days and in the fridge for 2 weeks.

tips: The two critical steps are: checking one-string consistency of sugar syrup and the final Mysore Pak consistency.
Switch off at the exact moment the mixture leaves the pan and forms a non-sticky ball — even a minute late will make it hard.
Keep the flame on low after adding besan to prevent burning.
The colour of Mysore Pak depends on the besan flour — deeper yellow besan gives a richer colour.
Ghee may ooze out slightly at the final stage — this is normal and will be absorbed on cooling.
Add ghee in portions, not all at once — the mixture absorbs ghee quickly.
Use a non-stick pan for easier handling and to prevent sticking.
Mysore Pak stays well at room temperature for 5 days and in the fridge for 2 weeks.
